draft.introduction: 2 Corinthians 12:1–10 recounts Paul’s mystical ascent to the third heaven and his subsequent “thorn in the flesh.” It pairs the highest spiritual experience with the deepest vulnerability, establishing a paradox of power in weakness.

translation.text: I know a man in Christ who fourteen years ago was caught up to the third heaven—whether in the body or out of the body I do not know, God knows. And he heard things that cannot be told, which man may not utter. So to keep me from becoming conceited, a thorn was given me in the flesh. Three times I pleaded with the Lord about this. But he said to me, ‘My grace is sufficient for you, for my power is made perfect in weakness.’
translation.original_text: οἶδα ἄνθρωπον ἐν Χριστῷ πρὸ ἐτῶν δεκατεσσάρων ἁρπαγέντα ἕως τρίτου οὐρανοῦ

translator_notes.note: Paul’s ascent to the “third heaven” echoes Jewish merkavah mysticism and apocalyptic ascent traditions (cf. 1 Enoch, 2 Enoch). The uncertainty about whether the experience was bodily or ecstatic reflects genuine phenomenological ambiguity in mystical experience.
